title: Use asyncio.gather for concurrent search_nodes + search in get_entity
analysis: MemoryService.get_entity() (lines 796-829 of memory_service.py) sequentially awaits two independent Graphiti I/O calls: graphiti.search_nodes() then graphiti.search(). Both are fully independent — search_nodes fetches entity nodes while search fetches edges (facts). Replacing the two sequential awaits with asyncio.gather() will reduce wall-clock time from t1+t2 to max(t1,t2). The asyncio module is already imported. The codebase has established gather patterns (durable_queue.close, task_interceptor drain, test helpers). The backend methods (graphiti_client.py) already wrap each call in asyncio.wait_for() with timeout handling and return empty lists on timeout, so no additional timeout logic is needed in get_entity. The task description mentions _search_graphiti() as another candidate, but that method only makes a single graphiti.search() call — there is nothing to parallelize there. The broader search() method already uses asyncio.wait() for multi-store fan-out. Therefore, the only change is in get_entity(). There are currently NO dedicated tests for get_entity() — the TestGetEpisodes class exists but TestGetEntity does not. This is an opportunity to add characterization tests alongside the concurrency test.

prerequisites.description: Add TestGetEntity characterization tests to test_memory_service.py: test_returns_correct_structure (verifies nodes/edges dict shape with mock data including uuid, name, summary, labels on nodes and uuid, fact on edges), test_calls_with_correct_args (verifies search_nodes and search are called with correct query, group_ids, max_nodes/num_results), test_empty_results (verifies empty lists return empty nodes/edges). These tests pass on current sequential code and serve as regression guards for the refactor.

steps.description: Write TestGetEntity.test_concurrent_execution: uses call_log list with async side_effects that append 'search_nodes_start'/'search_nodes_end' and 'search_start'/'search_end' markers with asyncio.sleep(0.01) between start/end. Asserts that search_start index < search_nodes_end index in the log, proving both coroutines were dispatched before either completed. This test FAILS on the current sequential code (sequential produces [..._start, ..._end, ..._start, ..._end]).

Replace sequential awaits in get_entity() (lines 802-811) with: nodes, edges = await asyncio.gather(self.graphiti.search_nodes(query=name, group_ids=[project_id], max_nodes=5), self.graphiti.search(query=name, group_ids=[project_id], num_results=10)). This makes the concurrency test pass while preserving all characterization tests.

Write TestGetEntity.test_error_propagation: set search_nodes side_effect to raise RuntimeError, verify get_entity propagates it (asyncio.gather without return_exceptions=True raises on first failure). Then test the inverse — search raises while search_nodes succeeds. Confirms exception semantics match the original sequential behavior.

Verify all tests pass (no additional implementation expected — asyncio.gather without return_exceptions already propagates exceptions identically to sequential awaits). Run full test suite: pytest fused-memory/tests/test_memory_service.py -v.

design_decisions.rationale: The current sequential code lets exceptions propagate to the caller (server/tools.py wraps in try/except). Using gather without return_exceptions preserves this behavior — the first exception cancels the other task and propagates. Using return_exceptions=True would require manual result inspection and would silently change error semantics.

_search_graphiti() only calls self.graphiti.search() — a single I/O operation. There is nothing to parallelize. The task description's mention of it appears to be a misread; the broader search() method (which calls _search_graphiti and _search_mem0 in parallel) already uses asyncio.wait() for concurrency.

design_decisions.decision: Use call_log ordering pattern for concurrency test instead of timing or patching asyncio.gather
design_decisions.rationale: Timing-based tests are flaky. Patching asyncio.gather is fragile and tests implementation rather than behavior. The call_log approach with asyncio.sleep(0.01) yields deterministic interleaving: concurrent execution produces interleaved start/end markers while sequential produces grouped markers. This is the standard pattern for testing async concurrency in unit tests.

design_decisions.decision: Add characterization tests as prerequisites, not TDD steps
design_decisions.rationale: Characterization tests verify existing behavior and pass immediately — they don't follow the red-green TDD cycle. They belong in prerequisites as regression safety nets. Only the concurrency and error-propagation tests follow TDD (fail first, then implement).

reuse.what: Existing service fixture with mocked graphiti (search, search_nodes as AsyncMock)
reuse.how: Reuse the service fixture directly — it already mocks graphiti.search_nodes and graphiti.search as AsyncMock(return_value=[]). Override side_effects in individual tests for custom behavior.

reuse.what: MockNode and MockEdge dataclasses in conftest
reuse.how: Use MockEdge for edge results. For node results, create simple MagicMock objects with uuid/name/summary/labels attributes, since MockNode only has a name field and get_entity needs uuid, summary, labels too.
No new imports needed — asyncio.gather is available via the existing import.

Follow the established pattern of asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) for cleanup scenarios, but use bare asyncio.gather (without return_exceptions) for get_entity since we want exceptions to propagate.
